### Post by Toehead on 2006-12-17
I have a 1.33 Ghz 17" Powerbook with the ATI radeon RV350 Mobility 9600 card. I run glxinfo, and it says that direct rendering is enabled. Glx gears yields about 20 fps, so something is obviously wrong. Both beryl and Compiz yield a monochrome screen with different colored boxes for windows. no text is legible.

here is a compy of my Xorg.conf
> # /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#

### Post by fuoco on 2006-12-18
Hi. I have the exact same problem. Which card do you have in your machine and which machine?
Mine is the last iBook which has radeon 9550 or something like that. 1.42Ghz 14''.

I have recently made it to manually compile and install packages of newer mesa/dri drivers for r300, and my fps in glxgears are now up to 900. I thought - cool now it works. But upon trying planet penguin racer I get only 3 fps. 
I am yet to think about anything else I could try to see what's going on...

### Post by Toehead on 2006-12-18
Where did you get the drivers to compile? Any tips? 


The machine is a 17" Powerbook G4, with the RV350 Mobility radeon 9600.

---

### Post by fuoco on 2006-12-19
> **Toehead said:**
> Where did you get the drivers to compile? Any tips? 


The machine is a 17" Powerbook G4, with the RV350 Mobility radeon 9600.

Yes of course. I used the 'prevu' tool, see: [http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=268687](http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=268687)

And I fed it with the .dsc files from packages.debian.org for the libgl1-mesa-glx and libgl1-mesa-dri packages from the debian testing repository - because feisty has the same as edgy still.
In fact you can run:
```
prevu http://ftp.debian.org/debian/pool/main/m/mesa/mesa_6.5.1-0.4.dsc
```
and I think that would create the necessary packages for. Don't forget to follow the prevu guide for it's setup, and to install the packages after you compile them.

Please tell me how it goes for you.

---

### Post by Toehead on 2006-12-20
I installed the two packages, my glx gears are now over 2000 FPS and all the screensavers work!
Very exciting. Compiz and beryl now just crash hahah. Ah well. I will try penguin racer and see what I get.

### Post by Christoph85 on 2006-12-20
Hi! I have a Powerbook 5,6 with an ATI RV350 NP [Mobility Radeon 9600/9700 M10/M11] card. Since I manually installed libgl1-mesa-glx and libgl1-mesa-dri packages from debian.org, glxgears gives me 1400+ fps, too. ppracer and gl-117 (a flight simulator) both run smoothly in fullscreen mode. Anyway, when I try running beryl, it seems to load, but as soon as the  metacity window decorations are gone, my system either freezes completely (without any extra options like "EnablePageFlip" or "ColorTiling" in my xorg.conf) or X just crashes (with extra options enabled in xorg.conf).

This is the console output of ppracer

### Post by fuoco on 2006-12-20
Thanks guys... So this is pretty strange, you seem to have a much better situation than I have...
True my card is different, I think radeon 9550 - it's recognized as FireGL Mobility T2e in lspci. But I don't think that should be a problem.
Can one of you tell me exactly what he did to get the two mesa packages to backport and and install? Also I assume you use edgy and the official edgy kernel? Did you have to backport some dev packages of xorg too in order to compile mesa drivers?

### Post by Christoph85 on 2006-12-23
fuoco, I have experienced the problem you're describing myself. glxgears runs smooth for a few seconds, but then the framerate drops down to 150-200 fps and the animation looks choppy. I investigated a little bit what might be the cause by testing every single option in my xorg.conf. Here's what I found out:

```

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"ATI Technologies, Inc. RV350 NP [Mobility Radeon 9600/9700 M10/M11]"
	Driver		"ati" # setting this to "ati" oder "radeon" doesn't make any difference.
	BusID		"PCI:0:16:0"
	Option		"UseFBDev"		"false" #false: higher CPU load but more fps (+300), true: lower cpu load but less fps.
	Option		"ColorTiling" "on" # setting has no obvious effect
	Option		"EnablePageFlip" "true" #increased glxgears framerate by 400
#	Option "AGPMode" "4"   # extreme impact on performance. glxgears runs smooth for a few seconds, then framerate drops to 150-200 fps (instead of 1300-2400, depending on the other settings) and the animation looks choppy.
#	Option "AGPSize" "64"  # see above
#	Option		"AccelMethod" "XXA" # XAA: higher CPU load, but equal framerate, EXA: lower CPU Use, lower framerate. Best result: nothing set
#	Option		"XAANoOffScreenPixMaps" # even higher CPU load, but no impact on framerate
#	Option		"RenderAccel" "false" # setting has no obvious effect
#	Option		"DRI" "false" # setting has no obvious effect

#Actual settings result in highest glxgears framerate (around 2400 fps), but cause highest CPU load.

```

It seems to me that setting the "AGPMode" or the "AGPSize" option is responsible for the 3D-Acceleration behaving weird. If you haven't done so, you might try removing any options from your xorg.conf, like so:

```

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"ATI Technologies, Inc. RV350 NP [Mobility Radeon 9600/9700 M10/M11]"
	Driver		"ati"
	BusID		"..."
EndSection

```

By the way, an Asus centrino notebook with an ATI 9200 card I recently used had this problem, too.

Linux debian 2.6.18-3-powerpc64 #1 SMP Mon Dec 4 15:40:16 CET 2006 ppc64 GNU/Linux

default system GL libs are:

$dpkg -l | grep libgl 

ii  libgl1-mesa-dri                   6.5.1-0.5                       A free implementation of the OpenGL API -- D
ii  libgl1-mesa-glx                   6.5.1-0.5                       A free implementation of the OpenGL API -- G

ii  libglib2.0-0                      2.12.4-2                        The GLib library of C routines
ii  libglibmm-2.4-1c2a                2.12.0-1                        C++ wrapper for the GLib toolkit (shared lib
ii  libglu1-mesa                      6.5.1-0.5                       The OpenGL utility library (GLU)
